title: M/s Infosys BPO Limited v. Rajesh Kumar Sharma
keyArguments: The respondent-employee was terminated following a duly conducted domestic inquiry which established charges of data breach and unauthorized disclosure of confidential client information, in violation of the employment agreement and company policy.

The inquiry officer's report was based on documentary evidence including system access logs, email records, and witness testimonies, and the findings were neither perverse nor arrived at in violation of principles of natural justice.
The respondent was given adequate opportunity to cross-examine witnesses and present his defence during the inquiry proceedings, thereby satisfying the requirements of a fair hearing.

The Labour Court erred in interfering with the punishment of dismissal on grounds of sympathy rather than established legal principles, as the misconduct was of a grave and serious nature involving third-party client data.

The punishment of dismissal was proportionate to the gravity of the misconduct and the employer, being a private entity operating in the IT sector, had the contractual and statutory right to enforce its confidentiality obligations.
judgmentSummary: The High Court of Delhi set aside the award of the Labour Court which had directed reinstatement of the respondent-employee with back wages. The Court held that the domestic inquiry was conducted fairly and the findings of misconduct were supported by cogent evidence. The punishment of dismissal was found to be proportionate to the gravity of the misconduct involving unauthorized disclosure of confidential client data. The employer's writ petition was allowed.
judgmentText:
IN THE HIGH COURT OF DELHI AT NEW DELHI
W.P.(C) No. 11342 of 2022

JUDGMENT
Date of Decision: 14th March, 2023

Hon'ble Mr. Justice Sanjay Pratap Singh

1. The petitioner, M/s Infosys BPO Limited, has filed the present writ petition under Article 226 and 227 of the Constitution of India challenging the award dated 09.08.2022 passed by the Presiding Officer, Labour Court No. III, Karkardooma Courts, Delhi, whereby the Labour Court directed reinstatement of the respondent, Rajesh Kumar Sharma, with 50% back wages.

2. BRIEF FACTS: The respondent was employed as a Senior Process Associate with the petitioner company. On the basis of an internal investigation, it was found that the respondent had accessed and transmitted confidential client data to an unauthorized third party using his personal email account, in clear violation of the Data Protection and Confidentiality Policy forming part of his employment contract. A chargesheet was issued, and a domestic inquiry was conducted by an independent inquiry officer. The respondent participated in the inquiry proceedings and was represented by a co-worker of his choice. The inquiry officer, after examining documentary evidence and the depositions of witnesses, held the charges proved. The disciplinary authority, after independently reviewing the inquiry report, imposed the punishment of dismissal from service.

3. The respondent raised an industrial dispute which was referred to the Labour Court. The Labour Court, by the impugned award, set aside the dismissal on the ground that the punishment was disproportionate and ordered reinstatement with 50% back wages.

4. SUBMISSIONS: Learned counsel for the petitioner submitted that the Labour Court committed a grave jurisdictional error by substituting its own view for that of the disciplinary authority on the quantum of punishment. It was contended that the Labour Court is not sitting in appeal over the findings of the inquiry officer and cannot re-appreciate evidence unless the findings are perverse. Reliance was placed upon the judgment of the Hon'ble Supreme Court in BHEL v. Shyamji (2001) 1 SCC 411 and Regional Manager, SBI v. Rakesh Kumar Tewari (2006) 1 SCC 530.

5. Learned counsel for the respondent sought to justify the impugned award on the ground that the respondent had a clean service record of seven years and the punishment was harsh and disproportionate.

6. ANALYSIS AND FINDINGS: This Court has carefully examined the inquiry proceedings, the inquiry report, the order of the disciplinary authority, and the impugned award.

7. The domestic inquiry was conducted in accordance with the principles of natural justice. The respondent was served with a chargesheet, was given adequate opportunity to submit a reply, was permitted to cross-examine the management witnesses, and was heard in defence. The inquiry officer has recorded detailed findings based on email records, system access logs, and testimonies of the Information Security team members.

8. The Labour Court, while acknowledging that the inquiry was fair and the findings of misconduct were proved, proceeded to interfere with the punishment solely on the ground of sympathy, citing the respondent's long service. This Court finds that such an approach is not sanctioned by law. The gravity of the misconduct, namely unauthorized transmission of sensitive client data, strikes at the very foundation of trust reposed in an employee working in the information technology sector. The punishment of dismissal cannot be said to be disproportionate to such misconduct. The Hon'ble Supreme Court in Manager, ECIL v. B. Karunakar (1993) 4 SCC 727 has held that courts must exercise restraint in interfering with punishment imposed by a disciplinary authority.

9. Accordingly, the impugned award dated 09.08.2022 is hereby set aside. The writ petition is allowed. No order as to costs.
